</div> </div><p>Coach Jeff Blashill also noticed the support, raving about how it made a particular difference in their <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/15/blackhawks-end-season-comeback-win-louis-crevier-connor-bedard" target="_blank" >comeback win</a> over the Sharks in their season finale.</p><p>“[I’ve learned Chicago] is the best sports city in the world,” Blashill said. “We have, in my opinion, the best fans in the world. It’s amazing. I coached in a great sports city — Detroit is a great sports city — [but] this is unbelievable. I can’t wait to win with this group here because it’s such a great fan base.”</p><p>So did Hawks rookie Anton Frondell during his home debut March 31 — after playing his first four NHL games on the road.</p><p>“On the road trip, when we played in Madison Square Garden and different arenas, I thought, ‘Oh, [wow], this is pretty good,’” Frondell said that night. “But they said, ‘Wait until you get back home.’ I know what they mean by that now. It’s a pretty good crowd here.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" > <div class="TweetUrl"> <blockquote class="twitter-tweet"><p lang="en" dir="ltr">Jeff Blashill: "I thought our fans were awesome. It was a fun atmosphere to be a part of. Given where we’re at and it’s Game 82, it just speaks so much of our fan base." <a href="https://t.co/G49ZpkauBp">https://t.co/G49ZpkauBp</a></p>— Ben Pope (@BenPopeCST) <a href="https://twitter.com/BenPopeCST/status/2044628923191857658?ref_src=twsrc%5Etfw">April 16, 2026</a></blockquote> <script async src="https://platform.twitter.com/widgets.js" charset="utf-8"></script> </div> </div> </div></div><p>The business department was confident in the Hawks’ attendance because of a major draw beyond the on-ice product: centennial season celebrations.</p><p>Not only did more than 20,300 fans attend each of the four special nights, but there was a spillover effect on the season as a whole.</p><p>Advertisements for the centennial were ubiquitous around the city, from bus stops to billboards to O’Hare airport gates. Most people influenced by those ads bought tickets for whatever game best fit their schedule, regardless of its specific programming.</p><p>There also was excitement surrounding the new black alternate sweaters (those six games averaged 19,869 fans) and a nationwide surge in hockey interest after the Winter Olympics. A lot worked in the Hawks’ favor this season.</p><p>Those same advantages won’t exist next season. The Connor Bedard novelty factor has worn off, too. More than at any other point since the pandemic, the Hawks will be dependent on their on-ice product convincing fans to come.</p><p>Yes, they have worked hard to reach a younger generation and broader demographics of fans during the rebuild, which theoretically has expanded the pool of people exposed to hockey and interested in buying tickets.</p><p>Approximately 40% of ticket-holders this season were new fans, and the membership renewal rate for next season currently sits at 95%, according to the Hawks.</p><p>But it’s about time the Hawks start teaching those new fans about the satisfaction of winning — and start rewarding their longtime fans’ support.</p><p>The lack of noise during lineup introductions this season, even from full houses, revealed how little emotional connection fans have with Hawks players. And too often, those full houses went home disappointed by those players’ performance.</p><p>The responsibility to change that partly <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/12/blackhawks-vision-next-season-summer-improvement-connor-bedard-ryan-donato" target="_blank" >falls on the players</a>. It falls more heavily on newly <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/blackhawks/2026/04/15/blackhawks-kyle-davidson-contract-extension-danny-wirtz" target="_blank" >extended</a> general manager Kyle Davidson, who might not have many opportunities to bolster the roster from outside this summer but needs to be aggressive whenever they arise.</p><p>His success or failure likely will be seen more vividly in the Hawks’ attendance — and ticket revenue — next season.</p>
entries.author: Ben Pope

entries.title: Man fatally shot, another wounded during brawl in Chatham apartment
entries.summary: <p>Three guns were found inside an apartment late Wednesday where one man man was fatally shot and another was wounded during an argument in Chatham.</p><p>Around 10:40 p.m., several men were inside an apartment in the 7900 block of South Saint Lawrence Avenue when an argument became physical, Chicago police said. </p><p>During the fight, someone fired shots and struck two men before fleeing, officials said. </p><p>A 30-year-old man was shot multiple times in the leg and was taken to the University of Chicago Medical Center where he died, police said. A second man, 31, was shot in the neck and was taken to the same hospital in good condition. </p><p>Three handguns were recovered at the scene, authorities said. </p><p>Area 2 detectives were investigating. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: State police fatally shoot armed suspect during domestic incident in Woodlawn
entries.summary: <p>Illinois state police fatally shot an armed suspect during a domestic incident Wednesday night in Woodlawn on the South Side. </p><p>State troopers responded to the 6500 block of South Champlain Avenue around 10:50 p.m. for a domestic battery and encountered the armed suspect where "a struggle ensued in which shots were fired and the suspect was struck by gunfire," state police said. </p><p>The suspect was taken to an area hospital where they were pronounced dead, officials said. </p><p>A gun was recovered from the scene. </p><p>The ISP Division of Internal Investigation Special Agents are investigating the shooting. </p><p>No officers were injured. </p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Dear Abby: Sister claims late mother changed her estate plan in secret
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> My mother passed away recently. She suffered from dementia and was a cancer survivor, but she lived well into her 80s. It was difficult for me to witness her mental decline when I spoke to her on the phone or visited her occasionally in the nursing home. She lived near my sister, so my sister had a much closer relationship with her than I did.</p><p>Mom met with my sister and me several years ago to discuss her estate planning. Both of us were included as beneficiaries, as well as Mom's second husband. My sister has just informed me that, three years ago, Mom replaced the existing living trust with a new one making her the sole beneficiary. She smugly claimed that she was always there for Mother and that I was an absentee child.</p><p>I was stunned to learn this while still recovering from the loss of my mother. I contacted a trust attorney, who told me I could contest the new trust on the grounds that Mother had dementia when it was prepared and that my sister may have coerced her to make her the sole beneficiary.</p><p>If I pursue a lawsuit, it will undoubtedly drive a wedge between my family and my sister's family. On the other hand, doing nothing does not seem fair to my family. Please let me know what you think I should do. — <i>SON IN CALIFORNIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR SON:</b> I cannot decide this for you. What I "think" is that you should discuss this with your spouse (if you haven't already), decide how much you need the money you were originally promised and proceed from there. Regardless of what you do, your relationship with your sister will never be the same because of this.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I'd like to offer some advice to shy people like me. I didn't have a girlfriend until I was 30, although I had my share of female friends. (I met a couple of women in online chat communities.) I tried a bowling league in my 20s, but the bowlers were three times my age. Then I met someone online who introduced me to country music and country dancing.</p><p>Dancing wasn't just a fun activity, but I made a lot of friends, dated a couple of women and met my life's match. We have been married for 17 years now. If you don't know the dances, there are beginners' nights where you can learn. As a guy, I always had a numbers advantage because many women want partners to dance with. My advice would be to try it with no expectations. Abby, I hope this helps someone. — <i>STILL DANCIN' IN PENNSYLVANIA</i></p><p><b>DEAR STILL DANCIN':</b> So do I because your message resonates with me. Years ago, I had a wonderful personal assistant, Olivia. She was born and raised in the great state of Texas and had a love of all things western. Looking to get into the dating scene, one weekend Olivia went solo to a square dance, and that's where she met an "angel" who volunteered to dance with unescorted ladies. Long story short, they were married two months later. I was her matron of honor.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>Good advice for everyone — teens to seniors — is in "The Anger in All of Us and How to Deal With It." To order, send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Anger Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Federal Media Matters probe is an attack on free speech
entries.summary: <p>In November 2023, about a year after Elon Musk took over Twitter, Media Matters for America made a splash with a <a class="Link" href="https://www.mediamatters.org/twitter/musk-endorses-antisemitic-conspiracy-theory-x-has-been-placing-ads-apple-bravo-ibm-oracle" target="_blank" >report</a> claiming that the platform, by then rebranded as X, was "placing ads for Apple, Bravo, IBM, Oracle, and Xfinity next to pro-Nazi content." Musk responded by <a class="Link" href="https://www.reuters.com/legal/x-corp-file-lawsuit-against-media-watchdog-others-musk-2023-11-18/" target="_blank" >threatening</a> a "thermonuclear lawsuit" against Media Matters, while Stephen Miller, now a White House deputy chief of staff, <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/StephenM/status/1726281362108538955" target="_blank" >suggested</a> that "conservative state attorneys general" should investigate the organization for "fraud."</p><p>Texas Attorney General Ken Paxton and Missouri Attorney General Andrew Bailey took Miller's advice, launching investigations that were ultimately <a class="Link" href="https://storage.courtlistener.com/recap/gov.uscourts.dcd.263967/gov.uscourts.dcd.263967.37.0.pdf" target="_blank" >blocked</a> by federal courts on <a class="Link" href="https://storage.courtlistener.com/recap/gov.uscourts.dcd.263967/gov.uscourts.dcd.263967.71.0_1.pdf" target="_blank" >First Amendment grounds</a>. But after those threats fizzled out, the Federal Trade Commission began <a class="Link" href="https://reason.com/2025/06/12/the-ftc-risks-chilling-speech-with-its-advertising-boycott-investigation/" target="_blank" >its own probe</a> of Media Matters — a <a class="Link" href="https://storage.courtlistener.com/recap/gov.uscourts.cadc.42318/gov.uscourts.cadc.42318.2160563.0.pdf" target="_blank" >blatant assault</a> on freedom of speech at the center of a <a class="Link" href="https://www.courtlistener.com/docket/71146258/media-matters-for-america-v-ftc/" target="_blank" >case</a> that a federal appeals court <a class="Link" href="https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=B3c-nLLFBoI" target="_blank" >heard</a> on Monday.</p><p>To recognize the <a class="Link" href="https://www.cato.org/sites/cato.org/files/2026-02/Media%20Matters%20for%20America%20v%20FTC.pdf" target="_blank" >danger</a> posed by the FTC's investigation of Media Matters, you do not have to like the left-leaning group or accept its critique of X. Whatever the merits of the organization's advocacy, it is indisputably protected by the First Amendment.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-800000" name="module-800000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Columnists bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Columnists</h2><br><i>In-depth political coverage, sports analysis, entertainment reviews and cultural commentary.</i><br></div> </div><p>FTC Chairman Andrew Ferguson nevertheless seems to view speech that discourages businesses from placing ads on certain platforms as evidence of a crime. Ferguson signaled that attitude even before President Donald Trump picked him to run the FTC in December 2024.</p><p>When Marc Andreessen — like Musk, a major Trump donor — <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/pmarca/status/1855024291571790186" target="_blank" >complained</a> about "the orchestrated advertiser boycott against X" that November, Ferguson, then a member of the FTC's Republican minority, <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/AFergusonFTC/status/1855065186899742796" target="_blank" >agreed</a> that "concerted refusal to deal can violate the antitrust laws." </p><p>Touting his qualifications for the job that Trump later gave him, Ferguson<a class="Link" href="https://perma.cc/A56K-Q4YM" target="_blank" > bragged</a> about his "track record" of "standing up" to "the radical left" and promised to "investigate and prosecute collusion" on "advertiser boycotts."</p><p>As FTC chairman, Ferguson brought in several vociferous critics of Media Matters as senior staff members. And in May 2025, four months after Ferguson took office, Media Matters received a sweeping civil investigative demand from the FTC, which sought a long list of internal documents, including financial records and sensitive journalistic material.</p><p>The FTC, which has no authority to regulate nonprofit organizations such as Media Matters, initially did not explain the justification for those highly invasive and burdensome demands. But after Media Matters challenged the civil investigative demand in federal court, the commission said it was investigating "potentially unlawful advertiser boycotts."</p><p>It was not clear why the FTC thought Media Matters had any records relevant to that inquiry. And last August, U.S. District Judge Sparkle Sooknanan <a class="Link" href="https://storage.courtlistener.com/recap/gov.uscourts.dcd.281859/gov.uscourts.dcd.281859.34.0_1.pdf" target="_blank" >concluded</a> that the commission's putative motive, like the rationales for the Texas and Missouri probes, looked like a cover for unconstitutional retaliation against Media Matters.</p><p>Sooknanan issued a preliminary injunction against the FTC's investigation, saying "Media Matters is likely to show that retaliatory animus was the but-for cause of the FTC's CID (civil investigative demand)." Although the FTC is now <a class="Link" href="https://storage.courtlistener.com/recap/gov.uscourts.cadc.42318/gov.uscourts.cadc.42318.01208810356.0.pdf" target="_blank" >asking</a> the U.S. Court of Appeals for the D.C. Circuit to overturn that injunction, its arguments reinforce the impression that the commission targeted Media Matters because of the views it had expressed.</p><p>"Even publicly available information makes clear why Media Matters might receive a CID in an investigation into advertiser boycotts," the FTC's lawyers <a class="Link" href="https://storage.courtlistener.com/recap/gov.uscourts.cadc.42318/gov.uscourts.cadc.42318.01208770012.1.pdf" target="_blank" >say</a>. "For example, Media Matters' website calls for an advertiser boycott even today. Moreover, Congressional testimony has suggested that Media Matters participates in deplatforming and demonetization campaigns."</p><p>That supposed evidence "confirms that the FTC is simply targeting Media Matters for speech," the organization <a class="Link" href="https://storage.courtlistener.com/recap/gov.uscourts.cadc.42318/gov.uscourts.cadc.42318.01208822502.0.pdf" target="_blank" >argues</a>. According to the <a class="Link" href="https://tile.loc.gov/storage-services/service/ll/usrep/usrep458/usrep458886/usrep458886.pdf" target="_blank" >Supreme Court</a>, it notes, "the First Amendment protects the right to engage in politically motivated boycotts as a means of encouraging social change."</p><p>Media Matters says the FTC's investigation has hurt the organization financially by scaring away donors and deterred it from pursuing journalistic projects that might irk Ferguson or his allies. The chilling impact of Ferguson's vendetta belies his perverse <a class="Link" href="https://x.com/AFergusonFTC/status/1855065186899742796" target="_blank" >claim</a> that he is protecting "Americans' freedom of speech."</p><p><i>Jacob Sullum is a senior editor at Reason magazine.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-ae0000" name="module-ae0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Jacob Sullum

entries.title: Chicago shines with short movies about disabilities, finding community at annual Latino Film Fest
entries.summary: <p>Area creatives are leaning into local storytelling through short documentaries and stories that celebrate differences and community in the 42nd annual Chicago Latino Film Festival.</p><p>This year’s film festival, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/movies-and-tv/2026/04/12/chicago-latino-film-festival-movies-what-to-see" >kicking off Thursday,</a> features four locally produced films, including a short from the nonprofit youth media company Free Spirit Media, as well as a film that resulted from a collaboration between Deep Focus Entertainment and Mass Epiphany Studios. </p><p>In total, the festival spotlights 51 feature films and 31 short films from Latin America, the Caribbean, Europe and the United States. All screenings, including the opening and closing night features, are held in Lake View at the Landmark’s Century Centre Cinema, 2828 N. Clark St.</p><p>Here are four local short films at this year’s Chicago Latino Film Festival.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“Abel” is a 20-minute film about the life of Abel Berumen, a photographer whose life changed the day he woke up blind.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Brian Zahm</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>“Abel”</b></h3><p><b>Screening:</b> with <a class="Link" href="https://latidofilms.com/films/deaf/" target="_blank" ><u>“Sorda”</u></a> on April 17 at 7:45 p.m. and April 26 at 3:15 p.m.</p><p>Abel Berumen’s life changed when he woke up blind as a result of a rare genetic condition called pseudoxanthoma elasticum. Essentially, the transmission between his eyes and brain is being interrupted by the disease.</p><p>Years after losing his vision, Berumen met DePaul University professor <a class="Link" href="https://www.bzahm.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Brian Zahm</u></a> in an experimental filmmaking class and learned about his blindness and incredible photography career.</p><p>Zahm asked if he could gain access to Berumen’s archives to make a documentary about his life, and the 20-minute short “Abel” was born. Berumen was in his mid-50s when he got up in the middle of the night to use the bathroom and, suddenly, he couldn’t see anymore. “Everything was darker than dark,” he said.</p><p>For decades, Berumen worked as a commercial fashion photographer. He’d traveled to New York City and Miami for dozens of photoshoots and even opened a small studio in the Flat Iron building in Wicker Park for his personal work.</p><p>But being blind meant he couldn’t work anymore, and friends and colleagues began disappearing one by one. Berumen felt angry and alone, except for the one bright spot in his life: his partner of more than 30 years, Gia.</p><p>He’s since been able to see and experience life in new ways.“Life doesn't stop, it doesn't end,” Berumen said. “When circumstances change in one's health … It'll be different, it'll be difficult, but life is worth living and you keep moving forward [even] when it's still painful.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-1a0000" name="image-1a0000"></a> <picture data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago actor Benjamin Delgado (center) stars in Erick Juarez’s short film, “Cake.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided/Chicago Latino Film Festival</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>“Cake”</b></h3><p><b>Screening: </b>with “¡Basta Mamá!” on April 19 at 6:15 p.m. and April 21 at 8:00 p.m.</p><p>Erick Juarez’s short film <a class="Link" href="https://www.imdb.com/title/tt36286601/" target="_blank" ><u>“Cake”</u></a> stars Camila Banus as Rosy alongside Chicago actor Benjamin Delgado, who was five at the time of filming, as her son. The short film is a collaboration between local companies Deep Focus Entertainment and Mass Epiphany Studios.</p><p>The film is a 17-minute short inspired by the real story of director Erick Juarez’s 7th birthday. That year, Juarez’s mother decided that she’d no longer be baking cakes for her children’s parties. She (and everyone around her) got tired of burnt or undercooked cakes, so the logical solution was to buy one.</p><p>The short film, set in Pilsen, follows Rosy and Alex throughout the city as they head to a fancy bakery to get the last-minute dessert in an effort to make young Alex’s birthday the best one yet.</p><p>They end up at the real-life Bittersweet Pastry Shop & Cafe on West Belmont Avenue, where “Chicago Fire” actor Joe Minoso plays the part of the head baker.</p><p>He makes an exception for the mother-son duo on Alex’s special day, even though things don’t exactly unfold to plan.</p><p>“It is the story of me and my mom and how our community came together that day,” Juarez said. He said he hopes the short film reminds viewers of the importance of “kindness that we have for each other.”</p><p>“We know it, we just need a little bit of a reminder. We just need to see it,” Juarez said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-960000" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e29db4f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2115x1187+22+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F21%2F9e%2F337c6ceb405985eb0c0bc92ebaec%2Fusa-created-with-purpose.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“Created with Purpose” is a film about individuals on the autism spectrum in Chicago.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided/Chicago Latino Film Festival</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3></h3><h3><b>“Created with Purpose”</b></h3><p><b>Screening</b>: with “Comparsa” on April 23 at 5:30 p.m. and April 24 at 5:15 p.m.</p><p>Filmmaker Ashley Ephraim partnered with Tamika Lycheé Morales, founder of the Autism Hero Project, to tell a story that began developing over a decade ago.</p><p>When Morales’ son was diagnosed with autism in 2015, she blamed herself and “went through all the stages of grief.” There was also a sense of shame attached to the diagnosis. “I didn't tell those who were closest to me,” she said.</p><p>But after some time, Morales began to see her son’s diagnosis as a positive and wanted to help create an accepting, inclusive world for her son.</p><p>She founded the Autism Hero Project, which has since provided medical grants, competency training for first-responders and legislative advocacy. The organization hired Ephraim to work as a video editor for the Autism Hero Project’s annual galas.</p><p>Eventually, Emphraim’s film company BrightCap Entertainment helped make “Created with Purpose.”</p><p>The 20-minute documentary focuses on local autistic individuals and how their families, neighbors and law enforcement can help create a more equitable world for those on the spectrum.</p><p>“We wanted to redefine how people see autism without erasing the reality of it,” Morales said. “Because the truth is, autism is not just one experience. This documentary reflects that truth, and it pushes back against the stigmas that say that they're less than or broken.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ab76eec/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1920x1078+0+1/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7a%2F04%2F31625db54d01b0bab228f9bf7b2b%2Flluvia-still005.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“Lluvia” is a short thriller starring and written by Leiana Carrasco-Ramos.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Free Spirit Media</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>“Lluvia”</b></h3><p><b>Screening:</b> with “The Whisper” on April 17 at 8:30 p.m. and April 22 at 8 p.m.</p><p>Leiana Carrasco-Ramos wrote and starred in “Lluvia,” another locally produced film making its world premiere at this year’s film festival.</p><p>The 11-minute short tells the story of Lluvia, a young Latina woman working in corporate America. After getting passed up for a promotion, feelings of past trauma and prejudice begin bubbling up to the surface.</p><p>Carrasco-Ramos drew on real-life experiences with racism and fatphobia for “Lluvia,” adding that she wrote the script years ago during a “really angry time in my life.” “I felt really hopeless and trapped,” she said. “I feel like it's a big metaphor for letting out that aggression and that anger.”</p><p>The bloody thriller comes from Free Spirit Media, a West Side arts and media nonprofit for youth ages 11 to 25. Director Nathan Suggs and Carrasco-Ramos met through the writers’ cohort and agreed to work together on the film.</p><p>Ultimately, the film’s goal is to respond to workplace discrimination and silencing that young Black and brown women face daily, Carrasco-Ramos said.</p><p>“For the girls that are too afraid to get angry, who are too afraid to speak up, don't be,” she said, “because your voice matters.”</p>
entries.author: Ambar Colón

entries.title: Alexa Rice enters the beauty biz, on her own terms
entries.summary: <p>On a recent Thursday morning, Alexa Rice paces inside a West Side photography studio, adjusting accessories and stepping in and out of frame as models hit their marks for the camera. In jeans, zebra print booties, and a vermilion t-shirt, Rice holds an easy and convivial command of the room.</p><p>“Oh, I love your tattoos,” says Rice, eyeing one makeup artist’s arm. “Oh! Yours too,” she adds, spotting the videographer’s designs. “I feel left out. The way my mother would never talk to me again if I got a tattoo.”</p><p>All around the aspiring beauty mogul, the Humboldt Park space has been transformed into a canopy of greenery, the setting for a marketing shoot launching the 37-year-old’s new Beech Beauty brand. When it’s time for Rice to take center stage, she quickly locks in. Rice is good in front of the camera, too.</p><p>Nearby, her mother, Linda Johnson Rice, in an elegant navy set and notably tattoo-free, soaks in the scene. “There’s something about this room,” she says. “I can feel it. It’s like Alexa’s grandmother is here with her, smiling ear to ear.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0ac18f6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3986x2237+0+419/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbd%2F87%2F3b586c8d4345982ce11583128dc4%2Fi040405.jpg" alt="Ebony Magazine Editorial Meeting with Freda DeKnight, Publisher John Johnson, Editor Ben Burns, Leroy Wimbush, taken in their offices in a converted funeral parlor, Chicago, Illinois." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0ac18f6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3986x2237+0+419/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbd%2F87%2F3b586c8d4345982ce11583128dc4%2Fi040405.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0ac18f6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3986x2237+0+419/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fbd%2F87%2F3b586c8d4345982ce11583128dc4%2Fi040405.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>An “Ebony” editorial meeting takes place in the 1940s. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Chicago History Museum, ICHi-040405; Stephen Deutch, photographer</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>In a culture obsessed with beauty, Rice’s grandmother was a defining force in Black cosmetics and fashion. Eunice Johnson created the Ebony Fashion Fair, and Rice’s grandfather, John H. Johnson founded Johnson Publishing Company and became one of America’s first Black self-made millionaires. Rice was raised inside one of the most influential Black business dynasties in American history, and in a family that helped establish Chicago as a center of Black economic and cultural life.</p><p>At its peak, the Johnson empire included “Ebony” and “Jet”<i> </i>magazines,<i> </i>which together counted millions of readers; Eunice’s traveling runway show, Ebony Fashion Fair, that brought couture to Black America; and her cosmetics line of the same name.</p><p>Fashion Fair was one of the first luxury beauty brands created specifically for Black women in an industry that had largely overlooked them. But as fate would have it, Rice wouldn't inherit the brand, which at its 1980s peak was carried in more than 1,500 department stores nationwide.</p><p>That reality now leaves her trying to build something of her own, in a market that has shifted dramatically. Starting Beech Beauty, says Rice, is a way to "feel closer to my family, and my grandmother specifically. And it's a way to continue our legacy."</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-ee0000" name="image-ee0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/937d7eb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/450x253+0+39/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb0%2F4a%2Fc2457fc147b69efff2c34dd162a7%2Fimg-1658.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c66e660/2147483647/strip/true/crop/450x253+0+39/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb0%2F4a%2Fc2457fc147b69efff2c34dd162a7%2Fimg-1658.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5f5bf26/2147483647/strip/true/crop/450x253+0+39/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb0%2F4a%2Fc2457fc147b69efff2c34dd162a7%2Fimg-1658.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Alexa Rice (center) with grandparents Eunice Johnson and John H. Johnson. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5f5bf26/2147483647/strip/true/crop/450x253+0+39/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb0%2F4a%2Fc2457fc147b69efff2c34dd162a7%2Fimg-1658.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/09688ed/2147483647/strip/true/crop/450x253+0+39/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb0%2F4a%2Fc2457fc147b69efff2c34dd162a7%2Fimg-1658.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5f5bf26/2147483647/strip/true/crop/450x253+0+39/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb0%2F4a%2Fc2457fc147b69efff2c34dd162a7%2Fimg-1658.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Alexa Rice (center) with grandparents Eunice Johnson and John H. Johnson. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Alexa Rice</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>A front row seat to an empire </b></h3><p>As a kid, Rice — who radiates warmth and is quick to laugh, often at herself — didn’t fully register the scope of her family’s clout and impact. When asked about her grandparents, she starts small: her grandfather dropping to one knee so she could run into his arms (“I was a large child,” she laughs); her grandmother’s reading and spelling drills (“She was big on education,” she says); evenings spent watching “Murder, She Wrote” from the couch.</p><p>But Rice’s world was undeniably rarified, too. She remembers visiting the White House in 1996 when President Bill Clinton awarded her grandfather the Presidential Medal of Freedom. She had a precocious understanding of Valentino designs, and recalls wearing coveted Chanel flats to class at the University of Chicago Laboratory Schools. There were framed letters from the Kennedys and her mother’s annual Christmas party with a guest list that included criminal defense attorney Johnnie Cochran, all star athlete Bo Jackson, and future president and first lady Barack and Michelle Obama. Rice’s parents separated when she was young. Her father, André Rice, is the founder and president of a fund-of-funds private equity firm.</p><p>At one such party, when Rice was 11, she discovered she was allergic to cashews. Groggy from a heavy dose of Benadryl, Rice woke up to a familiar face.</p><p>“I said, who the hell is that?” laughs Rice. It was Michael Jordan, in 1999, at the peak of his powers, checking in on her. “He was around all the time.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-320000" name="image-320000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/feb0b27/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2077x1166+0+119/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb1%2Fb4%2Fc4925a4845229a618c8858b5bde1%2Fphoto-alexa-and-papa-oct-90-mar-28-2025-19-59-p1.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c0cb6d7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2077x1166+0+119/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb1%2Fb4%2Fc4925a4845229a618c8858b5bde1%2Fphoto-alexa-and-papa-oct-90-mar-28-2025-19-59-p1.JPG 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/85e731a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2077x1166+0+119/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb1%2Fb4%2Fc4925a4845229a618c8858b5bde1%2Fphoto-alexa-and-papa-oct-90-mar-28-2025-19-59-p1.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Alexa Rice and grandfather John H. Johnson" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/85e731a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2077x1166+0+119/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb1%2Fb4%2Fc4925a4845229a618c8858b5bde1%2Fphoto-alexa-and-papa-oct-90-mar-28-2025-19-59-p1.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e3ab159/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2077x1166+0+119/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb1%2Fb4%2Fc4925a4845229a618c8858b5bde1%2Fphoto-alexa-and-papa-oct-90-mar-28-2025-19-59-p1.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/85e731a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2077x1166+0+119/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fb1%2Fb4%2Fc4925a4845229a618c8858b5bde1%2Fphoto-alexa-and-papa-oct-90-mar-28-2025-19-59-p1.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Rice with her grandfather, John H. Johnson, who founded Johnson Publishing Company and became one of America’s first Black self-made millionaires.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Alexa Rice</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Rice also witnessed the empire unravel at close range. In the early aughts, Linda Johnson Rice, who took over as CEO at Johnson Publishing in 2002, faced a fast-changing publishing industry. Print advertising declined, subscriptions dwindled, and the company struggled to adapt to the digital era. In 2010, Linda sold the company’s Michigan Avenue offices, one of the first major downtown buildings designed and owned by Black Americans, and once a visible marker of her family’s success. In 2016, Clear View Ventures, a Black-owned private equity firm, bought both “Ebony” and “Jet.”</p><p>In 2019, Johnson Publishing filed for bankruptcy. The remaining assets, including an archive of more than four million photographs, were put up for sale through a court-supervised liquidation. The entire episode “was a very, very difficult and painful decision,” says Linda. “Really gut-wrenching, personally and professionally.”</p><p>One loss, however, was particularly crushing for both Linda and Alexa.</p><p>Fashion Fair, Eunice Johnson’s pioneering makeup line, had been more than a business; it was a living extension of Alexa’s grandmother. In November 2019, as part of the bankruptcy proceedings, the brand sold at auction for $1.85 million to an investor group led by former Johnson Publishing executive and close family friend, Desiree Rogers, along with Cheryl Mayberry McKissack, another Johnson Publishing alum.</p><p>Alexa says she and her mother believed there had been an understanding with Rogers that neither would bid on Fashion Fair during the bankruptcy proceedings. The pair were shocked to learn that Rogers was part of the group that ultimately acquired the company.</p><p>Linda prefers not to dwell on the episode. “That’s done for me,” she says. “People are gonna do what they’re gonna do, and there are things you can’t control. I cannot let that drag me down.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-d80000" name="image-d80000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0d7311f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1989x1116+0+942/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F39%2F92%2Fceb860d1450e9badfd85e5e16a2a%2Fap921101096.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/029c7bc/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1989x1116+0+942/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F39%2F92%2Fceb860d1450e9badfd85e5e16a2a%2Fap921101096.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b8a36f0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1989x1116+0+942/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F39%2F92%2Fceb860d1450e9badfd85e5e16a2a%2Fap921101096.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="John H. Johnson, chairman and C.E.O. of Johnson Publishing Co., poses with his daughter Linda Johnson Rice, who is president of the company, at the headquarters in Chicago, Ill., in Nov. 1992. The firm, which publishes Ebony magazine, is celebrating fifty years in the publishing business. (AP Photo/Mark Elias)" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b8a36f0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1989x1116+0+942/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F39%2F92%2Fceb860d1450e9badfd85e5e16a2a%2Fap921101096.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/25078e5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1989x1116+0+942/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F39%2F92%2Fceb860d1450e9badfd85e5e16a2a%2Fap921101096.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b8a36f0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1989x1116+0+942/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F39%2F92%2Fceb860d1450e9badfd85e5e16a2a%2Fap921101096.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>John H. Johnson poses with daughter Linda Johnson Rice at the Johnson Publishing Co. headquarters in Nov. 1992.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>AP Photo/Mark Elias</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Alexa, however, is more emotionally forthright. “It was the biggest devastation and betrayal I have felt,” she says. “I could not believe it. I was heartbroken.”</p><p>At the time, Alexa was a graduate student at Harvard Divinity School — for a while, she considered joining the ministry — and found herself sitting with that grief. “Divinity School is not a bad place to receive devastating news,” she reflects. “If you’re going to crash out, I recommend being with people who are training to be priests, pastors, and rabbis.”</p><p>Asked for her characterization of events, Desiree Rogers, now CEO of both Fashion Fair and Black Opal Beauty, another cosmetics brand for women of color, responded in an email: “JPC no longer owned the company when we bid [on] it. We were surprised and delighted that our bid was selected and that it would remain in the hands of African Americans.”</p><p>Looking back, Alexa says the experience clarified something for her: she and her mother may have lost Fashion Fair, but no one could take away the years Alexa spent at Eunice’s side, or what her grandmother taught her about color, skin, and a deep, enduring love of beauty.</p><p>“You can own the business,” says Alexa, “but you can’t own my grandmother.”</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-130000" name="module-130000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3><b>Johnson Publishing Company: a brief timeline</b></h3><br><b>1942</b> — John H. Johnson founded Johnson Publishing in Chicago with a $500 loan and launched “Negro Digest.”<br><br><b>1945</b> — “Ebony” debuted; at its peak, the magazine reached millions of readers, becoming a central chronicle of Black life.<br><br><b>1951</b> — “Jet” launched. The weekly digest became known for its coverage of the Civil Rights movement, including the publication of Emmett Till’s open-casket photos.<br><br><b>1958</b> — Eunice Johnson launched the Ebony Fashion Fair, which raised more than $55 million for Black organizations over five decades.<br><br><b>1971</b> — The company opened its custom-designed headquarters on South Michigan Avenue, one of the few downtown buildings owned by a Black company at the time.<br><br><b>1973</b> — Fashion Fair Cosmetics was introduced, one of the first major beauty lines for women of color; at its height, the brand was sold in 1,500 department stores across the United States.<br><br><b>2002</b> — Linda Johnson Rice took over as chief executive of Johnson Publishing.<br><br><b>2010</b> — Johnson Rice sold the Michigan Avenue headquarters, marking the end of an era for the company.<br><br><b>2016</b> — “Ebony” and “Jet” sold in 2016 to a private equity firm as debt mounted.<br><br><b>2019</b> — Johnson Publishing filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy, listing millions in liabilities and including as assets its archive of more than 4 million photographs.<br><br><b>2022</b> — A consortium of foundations commit $30 million to digitize the photography archive and make it publicly available through National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Getty Research Institute.<br><br><b>2026</b> — Alexa Rice lays groundwork for her new brand, Beech Beauty, writing a new chapter in her family story.</div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Look good while doing good </b></h3><p>Enter: Beech Beauty.</p><p>The idea first came to Rice in her final year at Harvard, where she had been studying how disparities in green space shape city life. Rice wanted to find a way to connect those issues with the lessons she had absorbed from her family. So, as her final thesis project, Rice proposed a beauty brand that would donate a portion of sales to planting trees in underserved communities.</p><p>“My mom always says, why not look good while doing good?” Rice reflects.</p><p>The project was also inspired by Eunice, who raised over $55 million for Black organizations through Ebony Fashion Fair.</p><p>In 2023, Rice decided to give Beech a real shot. She chose to start small, with her own money, and from an office in her Gold Coast apartment. (Rice declined to say how much she has invested, but calls the amount “significant.”) Later this year, she will debut a line of lip glosses in seven shades, each named for a flower, along with a sweatshirt, both sold direct-to-consumer through the Beech <a class="Link" href="https://www.beechbeauty.com/" target="_blank" ><u>website.</u></a> And true to the original project, a portion of sales will go toward combatting environmental inequity.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-3f0000" name="image-3f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Starting Beech Beauty, says Rice, is a way to “feel closer to my family, and my grandmother specifically. And it’s a way to continue our legacy.”</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Courtesy of Alexa Johnson Rice</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“Alexa is not interested in just being an heiress,” says visual artist <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/art/2025/09/19/theaster-gates-art-smart-museum-university-of-chicago-unto-thee-rebuild" target="_blank" ><u>Theaster Gates,</u></a> a close family friend and steward of the Johnson Publishing library, which is housed at his Stony Island Arts Bank on the South Side. “She is thinking about ways she can have a significant life and make a significant impact in the world. It’s a beautiful thing when a person finds the balance between recognizing the history they come from and not being burdened by it.”</p><p>Building the brand has been a process of trial and error. “I have done everything myself. I’ve gotten comfortable sending cold emails and making cold calls,” says Rice. “The worst that happens is they either don’t reply or they say no — I’ll live.”</p><p>Rice's first move was buying what she calls an “obscene” number of lip glosses, and testing how they wore over time. When Rice figured out what she liked — moisturizing with good color payoff — she found a California manufacturer and started working on her own formula. Rice was especially careful about how colors read on Black and brown skin. “A vibrant purple is going to show up so differently on me than it will on someone who looks like Lupita Nyong’o,” says Rice. “I wanted the shade range to take into account as many skin tones as possible.”</p><p>It took multiple iterations, she says, to create a product that felt inclusive, playful and bold. “This whole clean girl aesthetic, it’s just not for me,” says Rice, laughing at the current fad of dewy, fresh-faced minimalism. “I don’t know her. I wish her luck. She’s in my prayers, but that’s just not who I am.”</p><p>The result is a saturated lip gloss with a nourishing, emollient base that feels high-end, packaged with a ’70s-meets-millennial flair.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-b50000" name="image-b50000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3df5f1d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2Fd1%2Fbae4997743ad9f1255129d672863%2Falexajohnsonrice-2604050012.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5f5bc9a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2Fd1%2Fbae4997743ad9f1255129d672863%2Falexajohnsonrice-2604050012.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b65886a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2Fd1%2Fbae4997743ad9f1255129d672863%2Falexajohnsonrice-2604050012.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Alexa Rice, founder of Beech Beauty, granddaughter of John H. Johnson and Eunice Johnson poses for a portrait in the Johnson Publishing Company library and archive at the Stony Island Arts Bank in Chicago, Illinois on March 12, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b65886a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2Fd1%2Fbae4997743ad9f1255129d672863%2Falexajohnsonrice-2604050012.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2b95703/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2Fd1%2Fbae4997743ad9f1255129d672863%2Falexajohnsonrice-2604050012.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b65886a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fd6%2Fd1%2Fbae4997743ad9f1255129d672863%2Falexajohnsonrice-2604050012.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>“I take the privilege, the uniqueness, and all the lessons of my upbringing in this family with me,” Rice said.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Connecting the past with a bright new future</b></h3><p>Rice jumps into the beauty market at a moment when a spin through Sephora or Ulta feels like a red carpet award show. And while the market is huge — projected to surpass $700 billion globally this year — it is also crowded with everyone from conglomerates to celebrities like Hailey Bieber and Selena Gomez.</p><p>And it’s hard for Black founders to break through. According to Crunchbase, a database that tracks venture capital activity, less than 1% percent of all venture funding went to startups with a Black founder in 2024. The brief surge of support for Black-owned businesses post-George Floyd’s 2020 murder has largely receded.</p><p>None of this deters Rice because, she asserts, “if I’m not going to bet on myself, who will?”</p><p>Part of launching Beech Beauty, says Rice, has been launching herself. Over the past few years, the affable, droll founder has built a sizable following on social media, posting videos that toggle between stories about her grandparents, makeup tutorials, research on urban infrastructure, and lessons she’s learned while building a brand.</p><p>In one video, Rice lines her lips while detailing a new city ordinance aimed at addressing environmental racism. In another, she models Bob Mackie designs from her grandmother’s closet as she narrates the history of Ebony Fashion Fair. (Her impression of Wendy Williams as an owl is also a delight.)</p><p>Across her platforms, Rice’s aim is clear: connect her family’s past to a new chapter that is wholly her own.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-620000" name="html-embed-module-620000"></a><blockquote class="tiktok-embed" cite="https://www.tiktok.com/@alexachristinarice/video/7275830860039851306" data-video-id="7275830860039851306" style="max-width: 605px;min-width: 325px;" > <section> <a target="_blank" title="@alexachristinarice" href="https://www.tiktok.com/@alexachristinarice?refer=embed">@alexachristinarice</a> Replying to @james Redlining is just one branch on the massive redwood tree that is environmental racism, but i hope this helps. I will definitely be revisiting this topic in the future becasue there’s a lot to say. Follow for more urban environment content! <a title="urbanforestry" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/urbanforestry?refer=embed">#urbanforestry</a> <a title="urbanforest" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/urbanforest?refer=embed">#urbanforest</a> <a title="environmentaljustice" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/environmentaljustice?refer=embed">#environmentaljustice</a> <a title="environmentalracism" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/environmentalracism?refer=embed">#environmentalracism</a> <a title="redlining" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/redlining?refer=embed">#redlining</a> <a title="treeequity" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/treeequity?refer=embed">#treeequity</a> <a title="forestry" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/forestry?refer=embed">#forestry</a> <a title="urbanplanning" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/urbanplanning?refer=embed">#urbanplanning</a> <a title="arborist" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/arborist?refer=embed">#arborist</a> <a title="arboristsoftiktok" target="_blank" href="https://www.tiktok.com/tag/arboristsoftiktok?refer=embed">#arboristsoftiktok</a> <a target="_blank" title="♬ original sound - Alexa Rice | Beech Beauty💄🌳" href="https://www.tiktok.com/music/original-sound-7275830918559583018?refer=embed">♬ original sound - Alexa Rice | Beech Beauty💄🌳</a> </section> </blockquote> <script async src="https://www.tiktok.com/embed.js"></script> </div> </div><p>From her apartment, set against a backdrop of taxidermy tiger skin (one of many expressions of her family’s shared fondness for animal print), Rice has begun planning Beech’s rollout for later this year. She will soon head to Los Angeles with her mother, who is developing several film and television projects based on the Johnson story.</p><p>A few years ago, Rice briefly considered moving to Los Angeles and building her business from there. The plan didn’t stick. “Chicago is where my family is, and it’s where I want to be,” she says. “My grandparents built their business here, so why can’t I?”</p><p>Any new endeavor, especially from a family like the Johnsons, will inevitably come with scrutiny. “There will be comparisons, there always are,” says Linda Johnson Rice. “But you don’t know if something can be a home run until you get up at bat. Alexa comes from a family that swings.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f10000" name="image-f10000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e4c9094/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F0c%2F32056bc14b6c8720c08a64a83803%2Fobamacenter-111625-007.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/4ddbd00/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F0c%2F32056bc14b6c8720c08a64a83803%2Fobamacenter-111625-007.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eab62e4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F0c%2F32056bc14b6c8720c08a64a83803%2Fobamacenter-111625-007.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The Obama Presidential Center is under construction in the historic Jackson Park neighborhood on the South Side, Wednesday, Nov. 5, 2025. The centerpiece is the museum building, clad in light-colored, New Hampshire granite." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eab62e4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F0c%2F32056bc14b6c8720c08a64a83803%2Fobamacenter-111625-007.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2223382/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F0c%2F32056bc14b6c8720c08a64a83803%2Fobamacenter-111625-007.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/eab62e4/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2500x1403+0+132/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F63%2F0c%2F32056bc14b6c8720c08a64a83803%2Fobamacenter-111625-007.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Theaster Gates’s forthcoming installation, a large-scale collage of Johnson Publishing images celebrating Black womanhood, will soon be on view at the Obama Presidential Center. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Both Rice’s Beech Beauty and her mother’s new projects arrive at the same time as Theaster Gates’s<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/art/2026/03/19/obama-presidential-center-contemporary-art-collection-barack-michelle-virginia-shore" target="_blank" ><u> forthcoming installation</u></a> at the Obama Presidential Center, a large-scale collage of Johnson Publishing images celebrating Black womanhood. In addition, a consortium of foundations has committed $30 million to digitize the enormous “Ebony” and “Jet”<i> </i>photography archive and make it publicly available through National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Getty Research Institute.</p><p>The timing feels almost serendipitous, with the resurgence happening seven years after the bankruptcy filing. After all, the Johnson Publishing case was only finalized earlier this year.</p><p>As the only Johnson grandchild, that history can, at times, weigh heavily. But Rice doesn’t frame it that way. “I don’t take the weight,” she says. “I take the privilege, the uniqueness, and all the lessons of my upbringing in this family with me.” And that, adds Rice, no one can buy.</p>
entries.author: Elly Fishman | WBEZ

entries.title: Record Store Day returns to Chicago this weekend with live bands, exclusive pressings and $1 deals
entries.summary: <p>Record Store Day is an annual tradition that, while celebrated around the country, gets special treatment in Chicago. This Saturday, expect more than three dozen shops in the area to fete the day with live events and fresh pressings, including a 20th-anniversary release from a-Ha and famed private recordings of John Coltrane’s band by musician Frank Tiberi in the ‘60s.</p><p>Chicago’s independent record stores know how to celebrate, often drawing lines around the block — some even starting Friday night. Here are 10 you may want to check out this weekend.</p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://www.beverlyrecords.com/home.htm" target="_blank" ><u>Beverly Records</u></a></h3><p><b>Hours:</b> 8 a.m. - 6 p.m.<br><b>Address: </b>11612 Western Ave.</p><p>With its 60th birthday next year, Beverly Records is an institution. Every Record Store Day the store opens its doors two hours earlier than usual; this year's special programming includes a visit from the cast of The Goodman Theatre’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/theater-stages/2026/04/08/review-ma-raineys-black-bottom-goodman-theatre" target="_blank" ><u>“Ma Rainey’s Black Bottom.”</u></a> The performers will kick off the day with a ceremonial needle drop of <a class="Link" href="https://recordstoreday.com/SpecialRelease/20160" target="_blank" ><u>a collection of Rainey’s blues hits</u></a> (among the exclusive titles on sale for Record Store Day) and a raffle of tickets for the show. Customers can also get White Castle sliders and 20% off used vinyl.</p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://www.bridgeportrecords.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Bridgeport Records</u></a></h3><p><b>Hours:</b> 10 a.m. - 10 p.m.<br><b>Address: </b>3251 S. Halsted St.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DW1tLCpERCA/" target="_blank" ><u>Enjoy live DJ sets</u></a> and check out a pop-up from Illinois-based Prairie Cannabis.</p><div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Dusty Groove, pictured here in 2024, will open at 7 a.m. both days this weekend. They’re selling any leftover Record Store Day pressings on Sunday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://www.dustygroove.com/record-store-day" target="_blank" ><u>Dusty Groove</u></a></h3><p><b>Hours:</b> 7 a.m. - 7 p.m.<br><b>Address:</b> 1120 N. Ashland Ave.</p><p>Doors open two hours early to sell hundreds of limited releases at this Ukrainian Village shop. Look for free gifts with every purchase, plus donut holes, coffee, kids’ toys and a morning magic show. Any unsold Record Store Day items will be up for sale on Sunday at 7 a.m.</p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/lauriesplanetofsound/" target="_blank" ><u>Laurie’s Planet of Sound</u></a></h3><p><b>Hours:</b> 9 a.m. - 8 p.m.<br><b>Address:</b> 4639 N. Lincoln Ave.</p><p>In addition to many Record Store Day titles, Laurie’s will sell a large selection of rare used LPs saved specifically for Saturday. WXRT personality Terri Hemmert is also rumored to be making an in-store appearance in the afternoon.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure The shop will welcome customers two hours early on Record Store Day this weekend.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://outofthepastrecords.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Out of the Past Records</u></a></h3><p><b>Hours:</b> 9 a.m. - 6 or 6:30 p.m.<br><b>Address:</b> 4407 W. Madison St.</p><p>Original owners Charlie Joe and Marie Henderson have been selling records in Garfield Park since 1963, opening up Out of the Past in 1986. Marie is still at the store, where her granddaughter Annisa Gooden carries on the family legacy 40 years into the business. On Saturday expect exclusive Record Store Day titles, plus a selection of <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DW_5CNnkQDm/" target="_blank" ><u>$1 used vinyl</u></a> starting at 9 a.m. Set aside some time to dig, and don’t be afraid of a little dust.</p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://pinwheelrecords.square.site/" target="_blank" ><u>Pinwheel Records</u></a></h3><p><b>Hours:</b> 9 a.m. - 6 p.m.<br><b>Address:</b> 1459 W. 18th St.</p><p>The Pilsen shop opens at 9 a.m., but owners will pass out numbers to anyone in line starting at 7:30 a.m. The website <a class="Link" href="https://pinwheelrecords.square.site/record-store-day-2026" target="_blank" ><u>lists exclusive Record Store Day titles</u></a>. Shoppers can catch live music — hosted by Pilsen Community Books — from Chicago bands Macie Stewart, Flowering and Mail. Plus, enjoy pastries from chef Becky Carson of Cerdito Muerto. If weather permits, look for a sidewalk sale with discounted vinyl.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a href="https://www.facebook.com/events/s/record-store-day-at-round-trip/871379172587777/" target="_blank" ><u>Round Trip Records</u></a></h3><p><b>Hours:</b> 12 p.m. - 8 p.m.<br><b>Address:</b> 3455 W. Foster Ave.</p><p>Celebrate Record Store Day in North Park with free coffee and donuts starting at noon. The shop also offers 10% off all used stock and is planning live DJ sets.</p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://www.shugarecords.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Shuga Records</u></a></h3><p><b>Hours: </b>9 a.m. - 8 p.m.<br><b>Address: </b>1272 N. Milwaukee Ave.</p><p>Shuga’s Wicker Park location opens early with free coffee and donuts, <a class="Link" href="https://recordstoreday.com/Store/7654" target="_blank" ><u>plus a commemorative beer glass</u></a> and other goodies for early shoppers.</p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://valshallarecords.com/record-store-day-2025-sat-apr-18-at-8am/" target="_blank" ><u>Val’s Halla Records</u></a></h3><p><b>Hours:</b> 8 a.m. - 7 p.m.<br><b>Address:</b> 239 Harrison Ave., Oak Park</p><p>The Oak Park store, founded in 1972, opens three hours early with live music in-store throughout the day. The day will be capped by a ticketed performance from <a class="Link" href="https://valshallarecords.com/josh-caterer-live-vals-sat-apr-18-at-7pm/" target="_blank" ><u>Josh Caterer of Smoking Popes</u></a> at 7 p.m. Local vendors include Kinslahger Brewing Company, Taco Mucho and Dark Matter Coffee on-site. In years past, customers have lined up overnight, so you may want to get there early.</p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://www.606records.com/" target="_blank" ><u>606 Records</u></a></h3><p><b>Hours:</b> 10 a.m. - 7 p.m.<br><b>Address:</b> 1808 S. Allport St.</p><p>Peruse Record Store Day pressings and exclusive vinyl releases at 606 with free coffee and live DJ sets throughout the day.</p>
entries.author: Morgan Ciocca | Vocalo

entries.title: Cash-strapped DePaul closes the curtain on Downtown's historic Reskin Theatre
entries.summary: <p>For nearly 120 years, the Merle Reskin Theatre has hosted everything from "Pygmalion" and "Life With Father" to "Mirror of Most Value: A Ms. Marvel Play," which opens mid-May for a two-week run.</p><p>What's playing after May? Darkness. And not the 2021 Andre Wright play with the same name, either.</p><p>DePaul University is closing the Reskin at the end of next month.</p><p>The university says it's the result of a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/10/17/fewer-international-students-are-studying-at-some-illinois-universities" target="_blank" >budget crisis</a> that has been aggravated by the Trump administration's immigration policies that has reduced the amount of tuition-paying international students attending the school.</p><p>Also part of DePaul's budget troubles: The decision in February to close the respected <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/museums-zoos/2026/03/02/depaul-university-art-museum-closure-fullerton-lincoln-park-students-faculty-letter" target="_blank" >DePaul Art Museum</a> at 935 W. Fullerton Ave.</p><p>"The university is working to determine the future use of the Merle Reskin Theatre," a DePaul spokesperson said in a statement.</p><p>But until that use is found, it's lights out for the Reskin, 60 E. Balbo Drive. The decision places in limbo a landmarked 1,400-seat Beaux Art playhouse designed by Benjamin H. Marshall, one of Chicago's preeminent architects.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-360000" name="image-360000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
