data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Black-and-white 1988 image of DePaul University officials standing on stage, with one behind a podium announcing its purchase of Blackstone Theatre." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/421d49c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3024x1697+0+154/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F54%2F66%2F60ed4a384825a53fa1f1c274a9f8%2Fblackstone-theatre.JPG 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d8d9a59/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3024x1697+0+154/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F54%2F66%2F60ed4a384825a53fa1f1c274a9f8%2Fblackstone-theatre.JPG 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/421d49c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3024x1697+0+154/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F54%2F66%2F60ed4a384825a53fa1f1c274a9f8%2Fblackstone-theatre.JPG" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>DePaul University announced its purchase of the Blackstone Theatre in a 1988 news conference on the venue’s stage.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Sun-Times File</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>"I'm sick about it," Jane Lepauw, president of the nonprofit Benjamin Marshall Society, said of the Reskin's mothballing. "I would pray and hope that some [theater] company would come along and claim it. But given the financial environment in general, I fear that it will be sitting there for a long time. It seems like it's really up for grabs for its future."</p><h3>Reskin ‘a wonderful asset’</h3><p>Built in 1910 as the Blackstone Theatre, the venue sits in the western shadow of the 21-story Blackstone Hotel, which was also designed by Marshall and completed in 1910.</p><p>Marshall and his firm Marshall & Fox designed scores of notable buildings around town, including the South Shore Cultural Center, 7059 S. South Shore Drive, and the Drake Hotel, 140 E. Walton St.</p><p>For much of the 20th century, Marshall's ornate Blackstone theater — with its columned entrances and mansard roof — and his elegant Second Empire-styled Blackstone Hotel were a swank brother-and-sister act that drew the best plays, conventions and events.</p><p>Legendary actors including John and Ethel Barrymore, Spencer Tracy and Helen Hayes performed at the Blackstone during the good years.</p><p>The venue closed in the mid-1980s and was bought by DePaul for its theater school.</p><p>A $2 million donation from businessman Harold Reskin and wife, Merle, who had been an actress, singer and dancer, helped refurbish what would open in 1992 as the Merle Reskin Theatre.</p><p>Merle Reskin died in 2023. Harold Reskin passed away in 1996.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-780000" name="image-780000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/57044b2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8081x4535+0+427/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2F9c%2Fc00b2ba34e17a4b1760bf70fd43c%2Freskin-2604160023.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/ff4338b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8081x4535+0+427/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2F9c%2Fc00b2ba34e17a4b1760bf70fd43c%2Freskin-2604160023.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/096995a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8081x4535+0+427/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2F9c%2Fc00b2ba34e17a4b1760bf70fd43c%2Freskin-2604160023.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Close-up of two columns on the facade of the Merle Reskin Theatre." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/096995a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8081x4535+0+427/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2F9c%2Fc00b2ba34e17a4b1760bf70fd43c%2Freskin-2604160023.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5071bca/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8081x4535+0+427/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2F9c%2Fc00b2ba34e17a4b1760bf70fd43c%2Freskin-2604160023.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/096995a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8081x4535+0+427/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F97%2F9c%2Fc00b2ba34e17a4b1760bf70fd43c%2Freskin-2604160023.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Architectural details enliven the facade of the Merle Reskin Theatre.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The theater's French-inspired auditorium remained intact, although it has faded somewhat in recent decades. The Reskin is home to DePaul Theatre School's Chicago Playworks for Families and Young Audiences.</p><p>DePaul said the Chicago Playworks series will continue this fall at the Theatre School building in Lincoln Park.</p><p>"That DePaul, which is really such a great school and it's got a great theater department, will be bereft of this wonderful asset," Lepauw said of the Reskin, "l think it's just so sad."</p><h3>What’s next?</h3><p>Whatever the Reskin's fate, it won't be demolition. The theater is listed as a contributing structure in the landmark-protected 12-block long Historic Michigan Boulevard District.</p><p>For DePaul, money is tight. But tight is relative: The university managed to come up with enough scratch to start construction this year on a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2025/11/20/plan-commission-approves-depaul-practice-facility-morgan-park-commons" >$42 million</a> basketball and athletics center on its Lincoln Park campus.</p><p>It should be equally diligent in finding the funds to preserve, restore and reuse the Reskin. The city shouldn't lose a live entertainment venue anywhere, particularly Downtown.</p><p>And Chicago certainly doesn't need a dark, unused building glowering over Balbo Drive.</p><p>But suppose DePaul finds it no longer wants the Reskin. Then finding a new owner who can restore the theater and put it in league with Downtown's Chicago, Nederlander and Cadillac Palace theaters becomes paramount.</p><p>Rich Gamble, interim CEO of the Chicago Loop Alliance, said his group has been talking to DePaul about the Reskin. The university is a member of the downtown organization.</p><p>"I'm not in a position to explicitly state the nature of those conversations," Gamble said. "We're a supporter, an advocate and a promoter of what we would like to see happen, which is a vibrant district."</p>
entries.author: Lee Bey

entries.title: Chicago’s school board election is coming. Here’s what to know and what’s at stake.
entries.summary: <p>Soon, voters will elect all 21 members of the Chicago School Board for the first time.</p><p>It’s a significant shift for the governing body, which was under mayoral control for decades. The city <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/chicago-school-board/2024/06/04/what-you-need-to-know-about-chicagos-elected-school-board" target="_blank" ><u>held its first-ever school board elections</u></a> in 2024, but that year only 10 seats were up for grabs. The rest, including the board president, were appointed by Mayor Brandon Johnson.</p><p>The change marks the culmination of years of work by advocates who pushed for an elected board, arguing that it would inject democracy and community voice into the board’s decision-making. Though there are concerns that <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/chicago-school-board/2026/02/19/big-money-expected-to-flow-in-novembers-historic-chicago-school-board-race" target="_blank" ><u>big money expected to flow</u></a> in this year’s races will undermine that.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-floatLeft><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-dd0000" name="module-dd0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><hr><h3>Tell us your questions for Chicago’s school board candidates!</h3><br>Whether you’re a Chicago Public Schools parent or a Chicago taxpayer, the future of our city’s school system affects you.<br><br>That’s why WBEZ, the Chicago Sun-Times and Chalkbeat Chicago want to know what issues matter the most to you — and what questions you have for school board candidates. Your responses will shape our community-driven elected school board voter guide, to be released this fall.<br><br><a class="Link" href="https://airtable.com/appu9nu8BNBrotoOR/pagRg1TV9UI2TfPGd/form" target="_blank" ><u>Take the survey here.</u></a><br><br><a class="Link" href="https://airtable.com/appu9nu8BNBrotoOR/pag9iBoUzH3Dpvjlw/form" target="_blank" ><u>También está disponible en español aquí.</u></a><br><hr></div> </div><p>There’s also <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/2025/12/08/few-residents-know-chicago-will-have-a-fully-elected-school-board-poll-finds" target="_blank" ><u>gaps in voter awareness to overcome</u></a>. Two out of three Chicago residents didn’t know the board would become fully elected, according to a poll by the nonprofit Kids First Chicago. And many voters don’t know exactly what the school board does.</p><p>Here’s what you need to know about the board’s responsibilities and what to expect in the elections later this year.</p><h3>What does the school board do?</h3><p>The board has several responsibilities, including opening and closing schools, hiring and evaluating the CPS CEO, approving the budget, the school calendar and vendor contracts and deciding which school buildings get upgrades.</p><p>They meet at least twice a month at their headquarters in the Loop and can expect to spend potentially more than 30 hours a month on their duties, according to the board website. But members aren’t paid to serve on the board. Some have raised concerns that lack of compensation discourages parents from low-income households from representing their communities.</p><p>The board also sets academic goals and priorities for the school year. That means members spend time attending community events, hosting office hours and visiting schools to get a sense of what their constituents want.</p><h3>How will the election work?</h3><p>For the upcoming election on Nov. 3, the city has been divided into 20 subdistricts. Residents will vote for one candidate to represent the area where they live. (<a class="Link" href="https://www.cps.edu/about/chicago-board-of-education/member-map/" target="_blank" ><u>You can look up your school board subdistrict here</u></a>.) But everyone will be able to vote for the school board president, which is a citywide seat.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.cps.edu/sites/cps-policy-rules/bylaws/chapter-1/1-3/" target="_blank" ><u>The terms of the newly elected members</u></a> aren’t all the same because of the board’s transition from being partially elected to fully elected.</p><p>Candidates who win races for seats currently held by appointed members will serve two-year terms. Those who win seats currently held by elected members will serve four-year terms. The board president will serve for four years.</p><p>From there on, elections will be held every two years so only half the seats are up for election at a time.</p><h3>When will I know who’s running for the school board?</h3><p>The window for interested candidates to get their names on the ballot is open. Each elected board member must live within the subdistrict they represent. Board member candidates need 500 to 1,500 valid signatures to enter the race. Those running for school board president have to submit at least 2,500.</p><p>Interested candidates began collecting signatures on Feb. 24. They have to submit their paperwork by May 26, at which point voters will know who the candidates are, though <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/education/chicago-school-board/2024/07/02/more-than-half-of-chicago-school-board-candidates-face-ballot-challenges" target="_blank" ><u>some will likely face ballot challenges</u></a>.</p><p>The list of candidates will be finalized by Aug. 27, when the county clerk must certify the election ballot.</p><h3>Will campaign contributions be important in this election?</h3><p><a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/education/chicago-school-board/2026/02/19/big-money-expected-to-flow-in-novembers-historic-chicago-school-board-race" target="_blank" ><u>Many expect spending</u></a> in this year’s school board elections to exceed what was spent on the first-ever school board race in 2024. That came out to $13 million, <a class="Link" href="https://www.chalkbeat.org/chicago/2025/01/27/ctu-incs-spent-millions-on-school-board-races/" target="_blank" ><u>according to a Chalkbeat Chicago analysis</u></a>.</p><p>But with twice as many district seats at stake this year, plus a citywide campaign for board president, many more dollars may pour in.</p><p>Labor-backed and pro-charter school factions are expected to play a role like they did in the 2024 elections. The business community, which has come out strongly against Mayor Brandon Johnson and his allies on the school board, could also factor in.</p>
entries.author: Emmanuel Camarillo

entries.title: Car insurance premium inequities are driven by age, credit and ZIP code, study finds
entries.summary: <p>It's well known that auto insurance companies charge more to people who have caused crashes or have had a DUI.</p><p>But the companies also charge more to customers over other factors they have less control over: age, credit and their ZIP code.</p><p>Those factors play a large role in customers' premiums and disproportionately affect older adults, low-income and working families, according to a new study commissioned by the Illinois secretary of state's office.</p><p>The study, released Thursday, found:<br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-afe8fb62-38df-11f1-bbb7-7d41cfb5a997"><li>Drivers with poor credit can pay more than 2.7 times higher premiums than someone with excellent credit.</li><li>Older adults can face substantially higher rates despite a safe driving record. Drivers ages 80 to 84 pay up to 72% more for bodily injury coverage than drivers in their mid‑50s.</li><li>Drivers in certain ZIP codes can pay more than 2.5 times higher rates than those in other parts of the state, even when all other factors are the same.</li></ul><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dd/09/a0718bd8456e99c06d22959e5d82/orcaa-auto-insurance-rates-study.pdf" target="_blank" >study</a>, conducted by New York-based auditing company O'Neil Risk Consulting & Algorithmic Auditing, backs up Secretary of State Alexi Giannoulias' push for a bill that he says would rein in car insurance costs that are unfairly discriminatory.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://ilga.gov/Legislation/BillStatus/FullText?GAID=18&DocNum=1486&DocTypeID=SB&LegId=160057&SessionID=114" target="_blank" >bill</a>, which passed the state House in March and is awaiting a Senate vote, would require the Illinois Department of Insurance to review auto and home insurance rate hikes over 10% and order refunds for customers it determines were overcharged. The state could contest proposed hikes if they are "excessive, inadequate, or unfairly discriminatory."</p><p>Every other state in the nation has similar laws to review rate hikes except for Illinois and Wyoming, according to Giannoulias' office.</p><p>"This study puts hard data behind what Illinois drivers have been experiencing for years — pricing practices that can penalize people for factors beyond their control," Giannoulias said in a statement.</p><p>A series of recent insurance rate hikes has spurred the debate over insurance premiums. Gov. JB Pritzker sparred earlier this year with Bloomington-based State Farm in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/illinois-governor/2026/01/23/pritzker-pushes-back-at-state-farm-in-escalating-fight-for-tighter-insurance-regulations" target="_blank" >calling for tighter regulations</a> after homeowner insurance premiums rose an average of 27% last summer.</p><p>Car insurance premiums in Illinois rose 18% in 2024, according to Giannoulias.</p><p>But car insurance companies argue that the bill would destabilize the state's insurance market and actually result in higher premiums. After the bill passed the state House in March, a coalition of insurance companies issued a statement saying it represented the "most sweeping and harmful insurance regulatory overhauls in state history."</p><p>The insurance companies noted that the bill ignores that auto insurance rates in Illinois are 18% below the national average. Rates are also declining in Illinois due to fewer crashes and claims, contrary to rising rates nationally, according to the companies.</p><p>"I think [the insurance companies] are scaremongering," said Abe Scarr, director of the consumer advocate group Illinois PIRG. His group wants to outlaw the use of race and credit, among other factors, in setting insurance premiums. </p><p>The House-approved bill does not do that, he said. At most, the state could use the "unfairly discriminatory" language to reject rate hikes that rely on factors like credit ratings, Scarr said. But it's not clear if that will happen.</p><p>"I haven't seen this used in other states for this purpose, so we'll see how that's used here," Scarr said.</p><p>It's unclear when the bill could receive a Senate vote in Springfield.</p><p>Giannoulias' study focused on how “multiplicative factors” in premium rate-setting result in pricing disparities.</p><p>For instance, someone living in the 60426 ZIP code that covers south suburban Harvey would pay a bodily injury premium more than 2½ times higher than someone living in the downstate ZIP code 62379 in Warsaw, all other factors being the same.</p><p>When considering age, the study found that a base rate bodily injury premium of $100 would jump up to $118 for a 70-year-old and up to $172 for an 80-year-old.</p><p>Giannoulias' study also includes nearly 2,000 responses from residents. He's been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/transportation/2025/07/29/secretary-state-alexi-giannoulias-aarp-car-insurance-price-discrimination" target="_blank" >collecting community feedback</a> over the last year through his office's “Driving Change” campaign.</p><p>Among those giving feedback was Algia W., an older adult from Oak Lawn. She said her family's premiums have gone up yearly despite them never filing a claim. Her insurance company told her that was due to the rising number of claims in her area.</p><p>"We are close to retiring soon, and there is no way we could afford this coverage on a fixed income!" she said. "Why are WE paying higher premiums because of where we live?"</p><p>A <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2019/5/17/18623979/car-insurance-roulette-higher-price-factors-illinois" target="_blank" >2019 Sun-Times investigation</a> found that being a woman, living on the “wrong” side of two adjacent ZIP codes, renting rather than owning your home, or working in an unskilled job could result in a higher price quote for car insurance.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2024/10/18/nonstandard-car-insurance-consumer-complaints-american-alliance-first-chicago-insurance-united-equitable-american-heartland" >Illinois’ most complained-about car insurance companies and how consumers can protect themselves</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p>
entries.author: David Struett

entries.title: Record rainfall floods homes
entries.summary: <p></p><h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p>🔎 <b>Below</b>: Record rainfall has swamped hundreds of homes — and meteorologists say more flooding could be on the way.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: More cops are accused of Paycheck Protection Program fraud, the ex-CEO of Urban Prep Academies is charged with stealing $100K and more news you need to know.</p><p>📝 <b>Keeping score</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-y/" target="_blank" >The Blackhawks ended their season with a feel-good win against the Sharks</a>, 5-2; <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-j/" target="_blank" >the Cubs bested the Phillies</a>, 11-2; <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-t/" target="_blank" >the White Sox fell to the Rays</a>, 8-3.</p><p>☎️ <b>Ask the mayor</b>: Mayor Brandon Johnson will stop by WBEZ's <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-i/" target="_blank" >In the Loop with Sasha Ann Simons</a> at 9 a.m. today to answer listeners' questions live on air. Call in 866-915-WBEZ (866-915-9239).</p><p>📧 <b>Subscribe</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletter-hub.suntimes.com/" target="_blank" >Get this newsletter delivered to your inbox weekday href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-h/" target="_blank" >Flooding from record-breaking rainfall invades homes, flash flood possibility continues</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-k/" target="_blank" >Zoe Singer</a></h5><p><b>Record rainfall</b>: Hundreds of residents reported flooding in their homes this week after record-breaking rainfall levels hit Chicagoland on Tuesday. O’Hare Airport set a record for April 14 rainfall with 2.43 inches, shattering the previous record for the date set in 1949, when 1.21 inches fell. Tuesday was the rainiest April day at O’Hare since it saw 3.54 inches in 2013.</p><p><b>Northwest deluge</b>: The Norwood Park area on the city’s Northwest Side accounted for 114 of the more than 600 flooding complaints logged by the city’s 311 service request hotline from midnight Tuesday through late Wednesday morning.</p><p><b>Flood watch</b>: A flood watch that includes areas around the Des Plaines River in Riverside and River Forest will remain in effect through Friday evening. All of Northern Illinois and Northwest Indiana are also under a flood watch, the National Weather Service said.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-u/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>SUN-TIMES SERIES 🌧️</h4><h4></h4><p>Flooding has been getting worse in Chicago in recent decades, according to a Sun-Times investigation.</p><p>Stronger, climate change-fueled thunderstorms are overwhelming the city’s sewers, and rain is falling harder and faster, backing up systems designed to allow water to flow.</p><p><b>Catch up on our flooding series</b>:<br></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 20px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-55e87a60-3997-11f1-b235-99df562e324c"><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-o/" target="_blank" >Flooding in Chicago is getting worse. Here’s why.</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-b/" target="_blank" >After a flood, mold sticks around and can make people sick</a></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-n/" target="_blank" >What can homeowners do to reduce flooding?</a></li></ul><hr><p></p><h4>LET’S HEAR FROM YOU 🗣️</h4><p><b>If your home was affected by flooding this year, how have you addressed it? Be sure to tell us the neighborhood or suburb where you live.</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Reply</i></a><i> with your answer (please include your first and last name). We may run your answer in a future newsletter or story.</i></p><hr><p></p><h4>PPP PROBLEMS 💰</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Jim Vondruska/For the Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-p/" target="_blank" >17 more Chicago cops bilked PPP loan program, inspector general says</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-x/" target="_blank" >Frank Main</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-c/" target="_blank" >Kade Heather</a></h5><p><b>New report</b>: Inspector General Deborah Witzburg said Wednesday her office sustained allegations against 17 Chicago cops in the first four months of the year for allegedly scamming COVID-era relief funds, and that the police department agreed to move to fire them. The claims appeared in Witzburg’s recent first quarter 2026 report.</p><p><b>Eight face firing</b>: Investigators dug into officers' Paycheck Protection Program loan applications — cash meant to keep small businesses afloat during the pandemic in 2020 and 2021. Fraud claims were sustained against eight officers, but the Chicago Police Department hasn’t decided whether to fire them. Another cop quit while under investigation. The alleged ripoffs involving those nine cops totaled $284,000, according to Witzburg.</p><p><b>Other accusations</b>: Witzburg’s report also alleged <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-q/" target="_blank" >a high-ranking official of a previous mayoral administration used their city title to get a job for their child with a city contractor, then tried to secure nearly $10 million in payments</a> to the contractor. A top aide for Mayor Brandon Johnson said the official referred to in the report worked for ex-Mayor Lori Lightfoot’s administration.</p><p><a class="Link" Derrick Curtis (18th) to replace Villegas as Economic Development Committee chair.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-z/" target="_blank" ><b>Subminimum wage</b></a>: The hourly pay of tipped workers will rise again to match the cost of living after City Council failed to override Mayor Johnson’s third veto. One month after freezing the phaseout of the subminimum wage at 76%, the Illinois Restaurant Association and its Council allies did not pick up a single convert. The vote to override Johnson’s veto was 30-19, four votes short.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-v/" target="_blank" ><b>ShotSpotter search</b></a>: rid of the controversial tool.<br></li><li><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-e/" target="_blank" ><b>Cab fare increase</b></a>: Alderpersons raised taxicab fares class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-yu/" target="_blank" >Immersive VR experience coming to Chicago lets visitors explore the Titanic</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-jl/" target="_blank" >Stefano Esposito</a></h5><p><b>VR sailing</b>: One hundred and forty-one years ago this week, the Titanic sank, taking thousands of passengers with it. A new virtual reality experience lets Chicagoans to strap on a headset and explore a digital recreation of that fateful voyage. Sun-Times reporter Stefano Esposito gave it a go. </p><p><b>What it's like</b>: "Titanic: A Voyage Through Time" is an immersive experience that gives you the sensation of being in a three-dimensional world while wearing a virtual reality headset as you traipse through a giant gallery space in Old Town. Guests start out exploring the wreck, and then they're led — by members of the crew — to different parts of the ship, above and below deck, as it makes its way toward disaster.</p><p><b>If you go</b>: You'll find the "Titanic" experience at 108 W. Germania Place, where creators hope to welcome "passengers" later this month. A ticket for the 45-minute adventure starts at $24.99 for adults and $19.99 for students, seniors and children ages 10 and older. It is presented in English and Spanish.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-jr/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p> </p><h4>WATCH: ABOARD THE VR TITANIC ▶️</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center><div class="Enhancement-item"> <div class="ExternalContent-wrapper" data-embed> <iframe width="113" height="200" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/36U0chlNQHY?feature=oembed" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen title="Immersive VR experience let's you explore the Titanic — a Sun-Times reporter tried it"></iframe> </div> </div></div><hr><p></p><h4>ON WBEZ 91.5 FM 📻</h4><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-jt/" target="_blank" ><b>In the Loop with Sasha-Ann Simons, 9 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-55e916a5-3997-11f1-b235-99df562e324c"><li><b>Ask the mayor</b>: Mayor Johnson stops by for his monthly sit-down with the show to answer listener questions, this time before a live studio audience.<br></li></ul><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-ji/" target="_blank" ><b>Say More with Mary Dixon and Patrick Smith, 10 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-55e93db1-3997-11f1-b235-99df562e324c"><li><b>Pope vs. President</b>: Guest Steven Millies of the Catholic Theological Union joins the show to discuss Pope Leo XIV’s response to a criticism by President Donald Trump. class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided/Chicago Latino Film Festival</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-ty/" target="_blank" >Chicago Latino Film Fest takes movie fans to Mexico, Venezuela, Cuba and beyond</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-tj/" target="_blank" >Ambar Colón</a></h5><p>The Chicago Latino Film Festival is back this year with an abundance of titles. Organizers say they are using the festival as a cultural bridge between Latin American countries and the U.S. by showcasing international cinema alongside local independent works for two weeks at the Landmark’s Century Centre Cinema in Lake View, starting Thursday and running through April 27.</p><p>After more than 40 years under founder Pepe Vargas’ leadership, the event is the largest and longest-running festival dedicated to showcasing work by Latino filmmakers in the U.S.</p><p>The first-ever feature-length animated movie made entirely in Mexico, "Soy Frankelda," and the opening night feature, "Aún Es De Noche En Caracas" from Venezuela, are two highlights among the 51 feature-length and 31 short films in this year’s festival.</p><p>Several local filmmakers are premiering shorts — written, filmed and produced in the city — at this year’s festival. <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-tt/" target="_blank" >Learn more about these local titles here</a>.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-ti/" target="_blank" ><b>FULL FILM FEST PREVIEW HERE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p>Yesterday, we asked you: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-th/" target="_blank" >Should Chicago continue to have delivery robots on the city’s streets?</a></p><p>Here’s some of what you said…</p><p>"I think delivery robots are part of our future. I support them. There have been very few glitches so far, and as far as I know, no one has been injured. The technology will continue to improve. Within a year or two, I doubt anyone will think twice about them." — <i>David Shiner</i></p><p>"Chicago should not have delivery robots. These food delivery jobs are typically the types of jobs that our youth would have as they were finishing high school or during the summer. Our kids, and adults for that matter, could benefit from these jobs. It doesn't seem very smart to allow bots to take over the workforce, no matter how small it starts. Additionally, the property damage will only increase, and soon, more people will be injured too." — <i>Dee Crawford</i></p><p>"Delivery robots and autonomous vehicles are just a way for tech companies to drain money out of Chicago and lay off workers. There is no benefit to the local economy." — <i>Mike McMains</i></p><p>"I would take more delivery robots on our sidewalks over the current situation ... our streets are routinely choked with double-parked delivery drivers. A drive along Lincoln Avenue between Webster and Fullerton any evening of the week is chaos!" — <i>Tom Stroud</i></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. Become a member to make stories like these free and available to everyone. Learn more at </i><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-widjuul-l-tk/" target="_blank" ><i>suntimes.com/member</i></a><i>.</i></p>
entries.author: Matt Moore

entries.title: Autoridades en Chicago obtienen la primera declaración de culpabilidad en un caso del Operativo Midway Blitz
entries.summary: <p>Los fiscales federales en Chicago aseguraron su primera declaración de culpabilidad relacionada con el Operativo Midway Blitz el miércoles, aunque no por el cargo de asalto que inicialmente se había presentado contra Anthony González Álvarez.</p><p>En su lugar, se declaró culpable de ocultar información sobre un crimen, admitiendo que reportó falsamente que su Ford F-250 había sido robada al Departamento de Policía de Chicago (CPD) después de chocar contra la parte trasera de un vehículo conducido por agentes de inmigración.</p><p>El cargo, oficialmente conocido como “encubrimiento de un delito grave”, es aparentemente lo suficientemente raro como para haber provocado una discusión en la corte sobre cómo pronunciar "encubrimiento".</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-c80000" name="module-c80000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“Nunca había oído hablar de eso”, les dijo la jueza del distrito LaShonda Hunt a los abogados.</p><p>Se espera que Álvarez, de 27 años, pague $1,362 en restitución, pero evitará la prisión bajo los términos de un acuerdo alcanzado con los fiscales. Sin embargo, Hunt primero debe decidir si acepta o rechaza el acuerdo.</p><p>Ha pospuesto la decisión por ahora y fijó la sentencia para el 22 de julio.</p><p>Álvarez está entre los <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/tracker-federal-prosecutions-chicago-status-trumps-immigration-blitz-ice" target="_blank" >32 acusados conocidos</a> que enfrentan cargos por crímenes no relacionados con inmigración vinculados al Operativo Midway Blitz, la campaña de deportación que conmocionó el área de Chicago el año pasado. Veinte de los 32 han sido exonerados y otros tres están en camino a que se desestimen sus casos.</p><p>Cuatro de los ocho restantes son miembros del grupo originalmente conocido como los <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/13/fiscalia-retira-los-cargos-de-conspiracion-contra-2-manifestantes-de-ice" target="_blank" >“Broadview Six”</a> y enfrentarán juicio el 26 de mayo. Otro hombre, Héctor Gómez, está programado para declararse culpable el 27 de abril tras ser acusado de cargos de armas relacionados con el Operativo Midway Blitz.</p><p>La administración de Trump utilizó afirmaciones de violencia "aterradora" hacia agentes federales para justificar un esfuerzo por desplegar tropas de la Guardia Nacional en Chicago el otoño pasado. Inicialmente fueron bloqueados por la jueza del distrito April Perry y, finalmente, por la Corte Suprema de Estados Unidos.</p><p>A pesar de la declaración de culpabilidad de Álvarez, nadie en Chicago ha sido condenado en una corte federal por un acto de violencia hacia los agentes durante el Operativo Midway Blitz.</p><p>Aún así, Álvarez admitió que a eso de las 10:30 a.m. del 3 de octubre, siguió a un vehículo de la Oficina de Aduanas y Protección Fronteriza (CBP) en el lado sur de la ciudad. Al girar a la derecha desde la 61th Street hacia Kedzie Avenue, chocó contra el lado trasero del vehículo de los agentes.</p><p>Más tarde ese día, Álvarez le dijo a CPD que su camioneta había sido robada, admitió.</p><p>Álvarez se declaró culpable a pesar de las preguntas planteadas anteriormente sobre el caso por su abogado defensor, Ricardo Meza. El abogado le dijo a Hunt en enero que "la camioneta que supuestamente fue golpeada ha sido reparada".</p><p>"Ni siquiera está en la condición en la que estaba cuando supuestamente fue golpeada por mi cliente, por lo que no podemos inspeccionarla", le dijo Meza a Hunt el 29 de enero. "Pero a pesar de eso, su Señoría, también queremos inspeccionarla porque queremos recuperar lo que se llama el registrador de datos de eventos".</p><p>Meza también dijo que solicitaría las notas de los agentes del Buró Federal de Investigaciones (FBI) porque "hay mucha confusión sobre lo que pasó, cuándo sucedió y cuántas veces fue golpeado un vehículo".</p><p>La asistente del fiscal estadounidense Stephanie Stern le dijo a la jueza que Meza tenía el poder de citar el vehículo él mismo, desde la compañía de alquiler de autos que estaba en posesión del vehículo.</p><p>"Está con Enterprise", dijo durante la audiencia de enero.</p><p>Cuestiones similares surgieron en el caso de <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/04/marimar-martinez-baleada-por-agentes-de-migracion-se-pronuncia-contra-asesinatos" target="_blank" >Marimar Martínez</a>, la mujer a quien le disparó un agente de la Patrulla Fronteriza el 4 de octubre. Poco después de que la acusaran de chocar contra el vehículo de ese agente, su abogado defensor reveló que el agente había llevado su vehículo a Maine antes de que el abogado pudiera inspeccionarlo. Los cargos en contra de Martínez fueron desestimados en noviembre.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Johnson calls Rahm Emanuel's mayoral record 'disqualifying' for a possible presidential contender
entries.summary: <p>As former Mayor Rahm Emanuel revs up for a 2028 presidential bid, Mayor Brandon Johnson on Thursday described Emanuel’s eight years as mayor as “disqualifying."</p><p>Johnson did not identify Emanuel by name when he talked about the one Democrat in the crowded field of possible presidential contenders whom he would like to, as he put it, “x-out.”</p><p>But there was no doubt about whose potential presidential campaign Johnson was attacking, even before Emanuel has formally announced. </p><p>“I have very deep concerns about the former mayor of the city of Chicago. What he did in Chicago — from school closures to privatization to austere budgets,” Johnson said during his monthly appearance on WBEZ-FM’s “Ask the Mayor” program before a live audience.</p><p>“There was a boy who was murdered by a police officer and it was covered up," Johnson added. "That’s disqualifying for me.” Johnson’s cover-up allegation a was a reference to the 2014 murder of 17-year-old Laquan McDonald by now-convicted former Chicago police officer Jason Van Dyke.</p><p></p><p></p><p></p><p>Dashcam video of Van Dyke shooting McDonald sixteen times while the teenager was walking away from the officer with a knife in his hand was withheld by the city until a Circuit Court judge ordered the city to release it fourteen months after the shooting.</p><p>Van Dyke was convicted of second-degree murder and aggravated battery. He was released from Taylorville Correctional Center in central Illinois in February, 2022 after serving less than half of his 81-month sentence.</p><p>In the furor that followed release of the video, Emanuel weathered weeks of protests and unprecedented demands for his resignation before ultimately choosing political retirement over the uphill battle for a third mayoral term. In an attempt to tamp down the controversy, he told the City Council a “code of silence” existed in the Chicago Police Department.</p><p> Johnson’s political broadside came a day after <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/elections/2026/04/15/rahm-emanuel-online-betting-prediction-markets-tax-proposal-presidential-bid" >Emanuel appeared</a> at a Wall Street Journal Live event in Washington and floated a 10% federal tax on online sports betting and prediction markets to boost revenue for science and technology. On Thursday, Emanuel refused to respond to Johnson's remarks.</p><p>“I’m not going to engage. Whatever,” Emanuel told the Sun-Times.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-690000" name="image-690000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fc11783/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1796x1008+3+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2F0d%2Fe37ead134b92a79cc002b96c06d3%2Frahm.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7540e63/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1796x1008+3+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2F0d%2Fe37ead134b92a79cc002b96c06d3%2Frahm.jpeg width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8d719a0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1796x1008+3+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2F0d%2Fe37ead134b92a79cc002b96c06d3%2Frahm.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Rahm Emanuel" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8d719a0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1796x1008+3+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2F0d%2Fe37ead134b92a79cc002b96c06d3%2Frahm.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/902d13e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1796x1008+3+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2F0d%2Fe37ead134b92a79cc002b96c06d3%2Frahm.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8d719a0/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1796x1008+3+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2F0d%2Fe37ead134b92a79cc002b96c06d3%2Frahm.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Former Mayor Rahm Emanuel critiqued Democrats and unveiled a plan to tax online sports betting and prediction markets during a Wall Street Journal Live event in Washington on Wednesday.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Screenshot</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Former longtime Inspector General Joe Ferguson spent much of his tenure at loggerheads with Emanuel. He reiterated Thursday what he wrote to members of the U.S. Senate’s Foreign Relations Committee who were deciding in 2019 whether or not to confirm Emanuel as U.S. Ambassador to Japan.</p><p>“That is not the fact” that there was a cover-up according to “an evidence-based record that was developed from many forms of inquiry and investigation,” Ferguson said. “And we’re better served not re-litigating controversies of the past..." </p><p>Ferguson said he was not surprised by Johnson’s attack on Emanuel with just a few months to go before Johnson must decide whether or not to seek a second term.</p><p>“In a heightened political environment, we’re always going to hear things like this,” said Ferguson, who now serves as president of the Civic Federation. “The problems of the city are of such magnitude that it’s critically important that all attention be focused on them because we have work to do.”</p><p>A pragmatic centrist who has never backed down from a political fight, Emanuel has long been in the cross-hairs of Johnson and his fellow far-left Democrats.</p><p></p><p>As a paid organizer for the Chicago Teachers Union, Johnson protested Emanuel’s decision to cancel teacher pay raises, triggering the 2012 teachers strike as well as Emanuel’s decision to close 50 schools in one fell swoop.</p><p>Johnson talks often about having “taken an arrest” while protesting Emanuel’s decision to close Dyett High School.</p><p>Last year, Johnson tried to undermine a potential political comeback by Emanuel — accusing the former two-term mayor of having devised and “executed” the anti-Black, neoliberal “playbook” that Johnson says is now being followed by President Donald Trump.</p><p>What rankled Johnson then was watching an interview with Emanuel and being “incredibly bothered by his temerity.”</p><p>At the time, Johnson said there has been a “long, sustained movement” in Chicago to push back against the “neoliberal agenda” that Emanuel championed to “set up austere budgets” targeting African-Americans and the “public accommodations” that support them.</p><p>“The playbook that Donald Trump is running is a playbook that Emanuel executed in this city,” Johnson said then.</p><p>“We didn’t get here because we just happen to have a tyrant in the White House. We got here because someone gave him the script… The shutting of schools. The firing of Black women. Privatizing our public education system is why the system is as jacked up as it is today.”</p><p></p>
entries.author: Fran Spielman

entries.title: Hyde Park Academy student was killed in shooting that stemmed from earlier dispute, police say
entries.summary: <p>Sixteen-year-old Eric Billups was fatally shot in an attack that left a Hyde Park Academy High School classmate wounded after the shooter and Billups, who had quarreled earlier, made plans to fight each other after school Wednesday, according to a Chicago police report. </p><p>When school dismissed, Eric and a 16-year-old girl were waiting at a bus stop in the<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/04/15/two-teens-shot-bus-stop-woodlawn-stony-island" target="_blank" > 6300 block of South Stony Island Avenue around 3:25 p.m</a>., police said. </p><p>"The offender confronted the victim at the bus stop, retrieved a handgun from his backpack and opened fire on the victims," according to police reports obtained by the Chicago Sun-Times.</p><p>Eric suffered at least five gunshot wounds to his abdomen, chest and left arm and was unresponsive at the scene. He was rushed to the University of Chicago Medical Center in critical condition and pronounced dead at 3:48 p.m., according to the Cook County medical examiner's office.</p><p>Eric's mom was too distraught to speak when reached by a Sun-Times reporter.</p><p>The girl, who was shot once in her calf, was also taken to the University of Chicago, where she was listed in fair condition. </p><p>Witnesses told officers that Eric and the shooter were supposed to fight after school, police reports state. It was unclear what the dispute was about, but the alleged shooter, who had been released from school early, came back still wearing a Hyde Park Academy uniform, according to reports.</p><p>Police found 12 shell casings from a 9 mm gun, two book bags and blood at the scene, the report said.</p><p>The shooting happened two blocks from Hyde Park Academy high school resulting in the school briefly being placed on a soft lockdown, officials said. </p><p>In a letter sent to Hyde Park Academy community members, Principal Rosette Edinburg noted that "some members of our school community were impacted by this incident" and the school was working to offer support services. </p><p>"We will be working with the CPS Crisis Management Unit to provide support to students and staff members. Our own school-based mental health professionals will also be available for students who need ongoing services," the letter states. "We are extremely saddened by this incident and will do everything we can to help our school community heal."</p><p>“All of us at Chicago Public Schools are saddened to learn of the loss of a young person’s life, and we extend our heartfelt condolences to the family, friends, school and community members impacted by this tragedy,” CPS said in a statement. </p><p>Fifth Ward Ald. Desmon Yancy said Thursday morning he was "still processing" the shooting in his ward.</p><p>"It's still difficult to understand how young people are dealing with trauma and grief while still having easy access to handguns," Yancy said. "It's hard for me to think about what goes through the mind of young people where they feel like they have to resolve conflict with violence." </p><p>Yancy noted that it's been a "rough few weeks" for the Hyde Park Academy community. </p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/03/22/girl-15-killed-while-riding-scooter-in-south-shore-hit-and-run" target="_blank" >Violet Harris</a>, a 15-year-old student, was killed after a vehicle crashed into the electric scooter she was riding last month in South Shore and <a class="Link" href="https://abc7chicago.com/post/family-mourning-hyde-park-high-school-student-killed-dolton-hit-run/18828745/" target="_blank" >Lania Smith</a>, 18-year-old senior, was killed in a hit-and-run in Dolton earlier this month. </p><p>"My heart goes out to the community and with community leaders about how we can not only support the students there, but also try to mitigate this violence," Yancy said. </p><p>On Tuesday, Yancy was in attendance at a "parent takeover" after "credible reports" of a teen takeover in Hyde Park from Mayor Brandon Johnson's office.</p><p>"It's better for us to try and find ways to support each other through this moment and at the same time figure out what role you can play in impacting young people so that this violence doesn’t become an option for them," Yancy said. </p><p>Johnson's office didn't comment on the shooting, citing the ongoing investigation. </p><p>No arrests had been made as of Thursday morning. <br></p>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: Larry Hoover's former Gangster Disciples No. 2 man says he's drowning in debt from court-ordered fine
entries.summary: <p>Gregory Shell was once the right-hand man to Gangster Disciples boss Larry Hoover in a $100 million-a-year drug empire, according to federal authorities.</p><p>Now, he says he’s flat broke. And he's asking a judge to wipe out most of what’s left of his court fine.</p><p>“It’s unfair,” Shell said in an interview. “The interest is more than my original fine, which was $15,000.”</p><p>Shell and Hoover both were sentenced in the 1990s to life in prison for running a drug conspiracy. They were shipped to the nation’s toughest prison, the Colorado “supermax” where the worst of the worst criminals, including former <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/07/03/chapo-margarito-flores-pedro-flores-saul-rodriguez-sinaloa-cartel" >Sinaloa cartel</a> boss Joaquín <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/el-chapo" >“El Chapo” </a>Guzmán Loera, are held.</p><p>Last year, they were sprung from the federal prison after winning presidential commutations on their federal convictions. But their legal troubles aren’t over.</p><p>Hoover, 75, is still serving a state life sentence for murder and is <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/04/07/gangster-disciples-larry-hoover-gangs-trump-eileen-burke" target="_blank" >asking Gov JB Pritzker for executive clemency</a>. after President Donald Trump commuted his federal sentence.</p><p>Shell, 68, is back at home on the South Side — and back in court, trying to get out of most of the remaining debt from his federal case.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-060000" name="image-060000"></a> <picture class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>In May 2025, President Donald Trump commuted former Gangster Disciples chieftain’s federal life sentence in a flurry of clemency actions.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>White House</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Former President <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2025/01/21/biden-larry-hoover-gangster-disciples-president-gregory-shell" >Joe Biden, in one of his last moves in office, pardoned Shell</a> in January 2025 and shaved $10,000 off his $15,000 fine.</p><p>Prosecutors say Shell still owes $1,461 in principal — plus a whopping $20,630 in interest on that — and argue that he shouldn’t be let off the hook.</p><p>Shell might claim to be broke now, but that could change, prosecutors said in a court filing late last month. They note they’ve got up to 20 years to collect on his debt.</p><p>Shell, acting as his own lawyer, has filed paperwork showing he doesn’t have a paying job and lives on food stamps and modest monthly Social Security supplemental income checks.</p><p>He says that, when he was sentenced, the judge didn’t check a box on a form indicating he needed to pay interest on his fine. </p><p>Prosecutors said the judge didn’t waive interest at Shell’s sentencing in 1998, so he’s required to pay it.</p><p>The decades-old sentencing form isn’t available for the public to see in court records.</p><div alt="Gregory “Shorty G” Shell in an undated photo. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8ddf1a2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1234x693+0+217/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F39%2F92%2Fd9ebbaa14194ae27e60e8f59a0ec%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-10-at-4-53-35-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9765952/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1234x693+0+217/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F39%2F92%2Fd9ebbaa14194ae27e60e8f59a0ec%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-10-at-4-53-35-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8ddf1a2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1234x693+0+217/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F39%2F92%2Fd9ebbaa14194ae27e60e8f59a0ec%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-10-at-4-53-35-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Gregory “Shorty G” Shell in an undated photo.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>At his sentencing, Hoover wasn’t fined. Not a dime.</p><p>But at least six of Shell’s co-defendants — also former high-ranking members of his <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/chicago-gangs" >Chicago street gang</a> — were hit with a combined $86,000 in fines.</p><p>Five have paid their fines and one remains in prison on a life sentence.</p><p>One of them, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2020/10/5/21502927/gangster-disciples-johnny-crusher-jackson-gentlemen-distinguished-nature-gang-first-step-act " target="_blank" >Johnny “Crusher” Jackson</a>, paid his $1,000 fine after he was released early from prison in 2020 under the First Step Act.</p><p>He also was ordered to pay $100 a month toward a separate $1 million forfeiture judgment he must split with another convicted Gangster Disciples leader.</p><p>The government seized $5,300 from Jackson at the time of his conviction and about $33,000 from his prison account to go toward that forfeiture judgment, court records show.</p><p>“With respect to Mr. Shell's situation, I think it is unfortunate that his original attorney apparently did not ask for a waiver of interest at the time of sentencing,” said Andréa Gambino, who has previously represented Shell and Jackson. "Now, the government's insistence on payment of interest that is 20 times what he owes on the original fine is counterproductive and puts a crippling roadblock in Mr. Shell's path to reintegration and success. </p><p>“This is especially unjust in light of the exorbitant price Mr. Shell has paid in decades of his life spent imprisoned.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div alt="Johnny Jackson’s interest on his fine was waived when he was sentenced in 1998. The judge checked the boxes on the decades-old form, which was attached to a court filing a few years ago." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fb57923/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2384x1338+0+102/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F85%2Ffe%2F874f73ca42278b69c5eb14fec8d9%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-10-at-4-36-13-pm.png 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/23d06a5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2384x1338+0+102/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F85%2Ffe%2F874f73ca42278b69c5eb14fec8d9%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-10-at-4-36-13-pm.png 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/fb57923/2147483647/strip/true/crop/2384x1338+0+102/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F85%2Ffe%2F874f73ca42278b69c5eb14fec8d9%2Fscreenshot-2026-04-10-at-4-36-13-pm.png" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p></p><p>Johnny Jackson’s interest on his fine was waived when he was sentenced in 1998. The judge checked the boxes on the decades-old form, which was attached to a court filing a few years ago.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>U.S. District Court</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Shell said Hoover wasn’t given a fine because the former Gangster Disciples boss was in state prison for more than 20 years for murder before he was charged with the federal drug case in 1995.</p><p>“Me being on the streets, I guess [the judge] felt he had to give me some kind of fine,” Shell said.</p><p>According to prosecutors, Shell regularly met with Hoover at Vienna state prison, where Hoover gave him marching orders for running the gang on the streets. The federal Drug Enforcement Administration hid microphones in visitor badges to capture Shell and others in the gang talking with Hoover in prison.</p><p>Asked whether Hoover’s supporters have offered to get him a job now that he’s free, Shell said, “I think about it a whole lot. Nobody really reached out to me. I am taking it one day at a time, trying to adjust to the streets after 30 years.”</p><p>As for Hoover’s request for clemency from the governor, Shell said, “I hope [Pritzker] follows Trump’s lead. He deserves to be home with his family like everyone else. I’m home. I’m not getting in trouble. I am doing the right thing. He will, too.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-750000" name="image-750000"></a>
